sentences.text: It must have been three, four weeks, maybe close to a month.
We, we were out of uniform.
I mean, there was nobody supervising us.
There were four or five or six of us, another officer from Indiana, and myself and three or four enlisted Marines.
And we just went around in loin cloths and went swimming in the lagoon at least for a while.
The natives spoke some Japanese.
Particularly, the kids, you could understand.
They spoke nice, clear Japanese.
And I remember swimming in the lagoon and one of the kids coming up to me and said, don't worry about the sharks.
They're only small sharks.
That stopped my swimming.
Notwithstanding what he had to say, I worried about sharks.
But my main activity was playing checkers with this other second lieutenant from Indiana.
And I think he beat me 300 consecutive games.
Yeah, but I wasn't as good a checker player.
We didn't fly back to Hawaii.
In some ways, some ship came by and must have taken us back.
I don't remember in detail.
It was a long trip.
It was 2,000 miles and any convoy you traveled in traveled at the speed of the slowest ship, which meant liberty ship's probably eight knots a mile--
--eight knots per hour.
Well, we-- Hawaii-- the island of Maui was our rest base for the whole division.

But we had an interesting set up because we-- I'm talking about the interpreters, the 10 or 12 of us-- we could detach ourselves from the division-- we had no assigned duties-- and go to Pearl Harbor and join the Navy there.
The Navy had a big translation center there called JICPOA, Joint Intelligence Center Pacific Ocean Areas.
And nobody supervised us there either, so we worked as long as we pleased.
And then at Pearl Harbor, we'd go to the nearest bar or the nearest beach or whatever and live it up.
It wasn't a bad life.
In fact, I like rest, rest, areas.
I didn't like the combat areas, but I liked the rest areas.
I saw a fair amount of it, yeah.
The next operation-- oh, the other thing we did at Pearl Harbor was we'd attend staffing briefings-- you know, projected next landing.
The next landing was a rather nasty one.
Roi and Namur in Kwajalein was not a big deal.
We lost about 300 men there, which was for a division of about 17,000 was not a heavy death rate.
Saipan was altogether different.
We landed in Saipan in June of '44, about a week after the Normandy landings.
And that was bloody.
We lost roughly half the division in casualties with a fair number of dead.
And there on Kwajalein, I landed D-minus one day, a day before the landings on an outlying island.
And fortunately, there were no Japanese on the island.
Otherwise, we would have been extremely uncomfortable.
In Saipan, I landed D-day, H plus three hours, which was not too bad and not too good either.
By the time I landed, the first couple waves had made the beach, but we were still getting some mortar fire and machine gun fire and sniper fire as we came in.
We were coming in on amphibious tractors, one of which next to us turned over.
But everybody was bailed out and rescued.
But Saipan was not a great deal of pleasure.
We went to Tinian, which was the next island three miles south of Saipan and took that island.
Well, Saipan lasted about a month, so it must have been sometime in July '44.
Tinian later became the base for the first atomic bombers.
The whole purpose of taking the islands were the airfields.
Both islands had airfields.
Yeah.
Also they were the nearest approach to Japan.
Saipan and Tinian were roughly 1,400 or 1,500 miles from Tokyo.
That's all I did.
I had interpreting prisoners, civilians.
There were a lot of civilians on Saipan.
It was a plantation island.
We must have interned 15,000 civilians who were the survivors out of probably 20,000 or 25,000.
I don't know how many.
We took quite good care of them.
We had a handful of prisoners, because the Japanese would never surrender.
My division must have had maybe a total of 30, 40 Japanese prisoners all together, whom I interrogated.
Most of them didn't know much.
They spoke freely, but they just didn't have any knowledge.
And then, we did the same thing.
I translated documents too, mainly for the entertainment of the troops.
Things like, we have 10,000 lives to give for the emperor, which people loved because it was so silly.
Occasionally, we'd run across some bona fide information and intelligence information.
But not much.
Back to Hawaii until January or February of '45 when we went to Iwo Jima.
I should say our casualty rate on Saipan was very heavy.
Between Saipan and Tinian, probably three out of every four officers I knew were killed.
No.
I never stubbed my toe.
I did cut my foot on the coral in Hawaii.
I got a bronze star and a Navy Letter of Commendation, two ribbons.
I wound up with three rows of ribbons.
I'm not sure I did earn them, but I got them.
I kept the order of battle for the enemy troops, which was very good because by the time the operation was over, we had a complete order of battle, if you know what the order of battle is.
I will.
Therefore, we knew whom we had been fighting and whom we had finished off.
Its value during the battle I think was very dubious.
The order of battle was maintaining an organizational chart for the Japanese forces.
What units are there?
Who are their commanding officers?
What kind of equipment do they have?
It was kind of detective work, because the Japanese didn't, didn't write this out.
We didn't have any good sources for it.
We picked up a little bit from prisoners, but the Japanese would generally identify a unit by the name of its commanding officer, the Yamamoto Unit.
The word was, I'm trying to remember, it was butai in Japanese, which meant unit.
You didn't know whether you were talking about a division or a platoon.
It could be any size.
You had to figure it out.
The dog tags reflected that too.
The Japanese dog tags would list the name of the unit commander and the x unit or whatever it was.
I was good at maintaining the order of battle.
I got it on Iwo Jima for maintaining the order of battle on Iwo Jima.
Yeah.
We went back to Hawaii.
We were next planning to land in Tokyo Bay.
We must have gotten back to Hawaii the end of March '45.
The Germans surrendered in May '45, and we were planning, as a training exercise, to take Wake Island, which the Japanese had taken at the beginning of the war.
And we figured that was a low casualty operation.
I should say, Iwo Jima was within 500 miles of Tokyo.
So we didn't have anywhere to go except Japan from Iwo Jima.

And we-- my division was designated to be the first backup division on the landings in Tokyo Bay, which would be absolutely the worst position in a landing that you can conceive because the-- with the assault, two divisions assault, would probably take heavy, heavy casualties.
But we were likely to take casualties in landing, and then we'd be the infantry that would do the fighting in Tokyo Bay.
So our expectations were not very good.
Well, several things happened, some of them scheduled and some of them unknown to us.
We knew by that time that the Russians were going to come into the war by sometime the first week in August.
We didn't know that we were going to drop atomic bombs in the first week in August, which we did.
Not by accident, the bombs were dropped a couple of days before the Russian entry in Manchuria.
And of course, by that time I had a good friend of mine.
He was a-- he was working on a doctorate in history in California.
We did an article probably in the spring of '45 for publication in which we tried to anticipate how the hell the war would ever end.
By June, the Japanese were looking for peace.
They were negotiating in Switzerland.
By June, the United States had taken the position that they had to unconditionally surrender, and the Japanese wanted to guarantee the position of the emperor, which finally we did do.
We maintained the emperor.
But we wouldn't accept the notion that we would commit ourselves to maintaining the emperor.
And that was the impasse in the negotiations.
The Japanese knew they were finished as soon as the Germans were finished.
I mean, there wasn't that great of a future in the Japanese war, but they were stubborn.
And our people were equally stubborn.
We wanted the, the rubric of unconditional surrender, so that was the hang up.
Some of this, we knew or guessed-- I'm talking about the two of us who were speculating in Hawaii.
We were trying to figure out how the hell we were ever going to get home.
And we did an article for which took a couple months to clear Marine Corps censorship.
Then, we got to and they were ready to publish the article, which was a damn good prescription for how the war actually did end.
And the editor of sent it back to us with a couple of suggestions.
We adapted the article to the suggestions, and it passed through censorship again.
And by the time it was about finished with censorship, the war ended, which obliterated the article.
That's how we occupied our time in between.
And when did the war end in the Pacific?
The war ended September 3, 1945.
That's my recollection.
Hawaii.
What was I doing?
I, I had defended some court martial cases, therefore the Marine Corps punished me by making me a prosecutor of court martial cases.
The effect of that was some court martials were still going on, and I stayed in Hawaii probably an extra three months, because they wouldn't release me from my duties as a prosecutor.
In those days, you got dismissed from the military by points.
And I think it took something like 130 points, which were measured in various ways, mostly overseas service, to get out.
I had something over 200 points, but I was stuck in Hawaii.
I also applied for a regular Marine Corps commission, which looked better to me than going back to law school.
And on conditions, I wanted to be an intelligence officer and study Russian and Chinese.
The Marine Corps said they'd be happy to accept my application as a regular officer, no conditions.
And I wrote back and said, thank you.
I have no greater interest.
The result of that was they put me on the rolls of a potential regular officer, and that cost me another couple of months.
So I didn't get out of Marine Corps service until well into '46 and just barely made the due date for my last year in law school.
I was assigned for almost two months to a Naval ammunition base at Crane, Indiana near Bloomington, which was another experience.
There I polished my shoes all day and rode in the jeep around to the guard posts.
And I was desperate to get out.
I mean, I didn't-- in some ways I liked the Marine Corps, but I had no great future in the Marine Corps.
January or February '46.
First Lieutenant.
I think Crane, Indiana.
She was working.
She was a psychiatric social worker by profession.
She was working for the American Red Cross at an Army base in Utah.
By the time I got to Crane, she was back living with me.
When I got back to the States she-- we resumed living together.
I knew something was going on, but I didn't know a great deal.
And I have to say my concerns were very narrowly personal.
My only concern with the European War was that it end, because it was a prerequisite for the end of the Pacific War.
But was the-- was the Holocaust, so-called-- a terrible term, I may say-- known to me at that time?
Certainly not in any detail.
I just knew that the Nazi's, as came naturally to them, were busy killing Jews.
Now, you mentioned that you left the Marine Corps in approximately January, 1946.
How quickly did you return to Harvard Law School?
Almost immediately.
I think the term started in February.
October.
At that time a divorce was underway.
I'm not sure whether the decree had been granted or not, but I was not living with my wife.
She was in New York, and I was in Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Went back to Illinois and studied and took the Bar Exam.
Between October and December '46.
Yes.
I had no well defined plan.
My biggest interest as a law student was international law, which obviously is not a great area-- in those days, in particular, was not a great area for private practice.
And I didn't want to go into private practice.
That was no part of my plans.
When I started law school, it was with a view to a government career in some agency which was very fashionable in the '30s, the Depression years.
By the time I got out of law school, the atmosphere was totally different.
So I was up in the air.
And not only that, I was restless.
I'd been through the war.
I wasn't ready to adapt to normal civilian life and go to an office regularly.
At one point, I talked to ex-Justice-- Supreme Court Justice-- whose name I forget.
He resigned somewhere in the middle '40s, who was-- Roberts, Owen Roberts.

US Supreme Court Justice, at the instance of a Harvard professor who shipped me off to see Robert somewhere in New England who was forming some kind of world federalist organization as a kind of an executive director or clerk or whatever the title would have been.
But it wasn't all that appealing, and I wasn't all that appealing.
So nothing emerged from that.
While I was at the University of Chicago Law library studying for the Bar on my own, there was a big announcement on a bulletin board there.
Anybody interested in war crimes trials please go to 5th-- not.
It might have been 5th Army Headquarters, which was not far from the University of Chicago.
And I was living about a block away from 5th Army Headquarters anyhow.
So I went to 5th Army Headquarters and said, I have good credentials.
And the Tokyo trials were just barely underway at that time.
I want to go to Tokyo and try the Japanese War criminals.
Obviously, that would not sit well with the Army.
I was too well qualified.
I knew some Japanese.
I knew a lot of Japanese history.
I'd spent a lot of time studying Japan, and I had a pretty good background, both at the University of Chicago and at Harvard in international law.
I'd had about two or three years of courses.
All of that registered with the Army and they said, oh, no, no, no, no.
That's not possible.
We may need people in Tokyo, but this guy can't go there.
We weren't fully staffed at Nuremberg.
Let's offer him a job at Nuremberg.
By that time, the main trial of Nuremberg was over.
It ended in November of-- no before that, probably, September, end of September '46.
So they offered me a job at Nuremberg since I was at loose ends and didn't know where I was going anyhow.
And at that time, I think a divorce must have been concluded.
And I had no idea where I wanted to work or what I wanted to do.
It seemed to me this was a good idea.
I could go to Nuremberg.
And I didn't know any German, of course, but and I wasn't current on the later war years.
But I would do a modest amount of work, draw a decent pay, and see all of Europe.
Yes.
Yeah I took the Illinois Bar before I went to the Nuremberg.
It must have been November.
With my mother, yeah, and brother and sister.
I didn't consult them.
By the time, I was a war veteran.
I was in my middle 20s.
I had been married.
There was nothing to consultant about.
They weren't going to have to finance me.
No.
I'd never been-- until the war broke out, I don't think I'd ever been more than 500 miles from home except going to Boston to law school.
Oh, I knew what it was.
I mean, I knew that it was an international trial of Nazi war criminals.
By this time, I had a pretty good idea of Nazi conduct too.
In those days I was a big reader.
Huh?
Oh, yeah.
I read lots of wartime and postwar literature-- magazines, newspapers, all kinds of things.
Subsequent proceedings.
Yeah.
Not in any detail.
No.
And I didn't ask either.
No.
I was a civilian by them.
I stayed a civilian.
No.
In fact, in retrospect I don't quite understand the recruitment.
This was done by a regular army sergeant or so at a desk taking notes.
My guess is, having gotten my application form, he probably shipped it off in some way to the Nuremberg people.
They must have approved it there.
No.
No.
It was the end of March or the beginning of April.
No.
No.
I packed as if going on a week-long vacation-- one suitcase and a handful of books.
And that was it.
My roommate, who was a close friend of mine from the Marine Corps days and had switched from European history to Japanese history, was studying German.
I looked at his text to see if I can make anything out of them, and I studied a couple of primers in German.
And that was about it.
No.
This was my roommate.
He was in the history department at Harvard when I finished my last year of-- in law school.
This was a fellow that probably my closest friend to that date and maybe even to this date who ultimately became a professor of the University of California.
Thomas Smith.
No, no, no.
He, he picked up a doctorate from Harvard in Far Eastern languages, and that's what he taught.
Not language, but history at both Stanford and the University of California.
With difficulty.
[LAUGHS] I took an airplane at Westover Field, Massachusetts which was-- I'm trying to remember how they labelled them in those days.
This was a kind of a standard army transport for freight plane, which had no seats for passengers, just steel bucket seats along the walls, no heat.
And we flew the Atlantic in stages.
We flew to Newfoundland, from there to the Azores.
And when I got to the Azores, some relatively high ranking military officers bumped me from the plane.
So I stayed in the Azore Islands for about a week.
And then caught another plane to Frankfurt, where I arrived in April.
I had no idea what conditions in Germany would be like at that point.
You read a lot of horror stories in the American press, but they were, as usual, grotesquely exaggerated.
I landed in Frankfurt and made my way to Nuremberg, and I, I can't remember how I did it.
I had no German money, whatever.
But I remember-- I know I took a train from Frankfurt.
I must have just walked on without paying anything or using a ticket.
I probably spoke only English.
I did speak only English, and I'm sure if anybody has any questions I denied that I could speak the language and that was it.
I left Frankfurt the same day I arrived.
I spent no time there.
I didn't stay overnight.
Yes.
I got to Nuremberg around midnight.
And across from the station in Nuremberg-- and all these places were thoroughly bombed out.
I mean, ruble was the main constituent of German cities, which suited me fine.
I walked to the Grand Hotel, which must have been a block or two away from the station in Nuremberg.

I knew what the Grand Hotel was, because earlier in the '30s I'd watch Nuremberg Nazi party rallies, which were generally centered around the Grand Hotel and then the big stadium which existed in Nuremberg.
I had a considerable amount of pre-war knowledge of what the Nazis were engaged in and specifics.
I went to the desk of the Grand Hotel.
I announced my name and said I was here for duty, you know?
The man at the desk said, I don't know anything about you.
You don't mean anything to me.
Why don't you try the hotel about two blocks away called the Deutscher Hof.
And I had all my luggage with me, which was one suitcase.
I had to walk two blocks over to the Deutscher Hof.
When I got there, there was a tall, good looking German who was there in-- had arrived-- and he spoke English-- who had arrived in Nuremberg as a witness.
I didn't think to ask a witness for who or what.
I was pretty damn naive.
And he wanted to know where he was supposed to be put up.
I think he was basically a Berliner.
And at the Deutscher Hof, they had a record on me.
They knew I was supposed to be there.
So they called me an army taxi.
And meanwhile, I was standing around the desk and this German was trying to find out where he was supposed to stay.
And they said, you're on your own.
We don't care where you stay.
Go find yourself someplace.
By this time, It must have been about 1 o'clock in the morning.
The fellow looked OK to me, so I-- as soon as they announced that I had a place, I said, come on.
Join me.
You can stay at my place, wherever it was.
And I took the German with me.
Then, in the morning we took a bus to the court house where I formally checked in.
And the bus was filled with Nuremberg prosecution staff people, mostly administrative people, interpreters.
They all looked at us as if at least I was crazy, you know.
What was I doing with this German on the American bus?
So I became-- all of a sudden it occurred to me it might have been a defense witness.
I did want to be accommodating defense witnesses.
So I spent some time checking up on him.
He was an extremely distinguished physicist by name Robert Havemann, who later was a director of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute and may have along the line gotten a Nobel Prize.
He had been picked up by the-- he was-- he was a witness for the prosecution, which made me feel a little bit better.
He'd been picked up by the Nazis and stuck in a concentration camp towards the end of the war because he was a bonafide-- a spy for both the British and the Russians.
And the only reason the Nazis didn't kill him was they wanted him to conduct experiments on nuclear projects for them.
That kept him alive.
I never had any further contact with him, but I did put them up overnight.
